planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring while soil is cool; in zone 9 plant September–February for establishment before summer heat. Gold Heart bleeding heart is spring-ephemeral — the plant emerges from dormancy in late winter or early spring, blooms in April–May, and then goes completely dormant as summer heat arrives, disappearing from the garden until the following spring. In zone 9 the summer dormancy occurs earlier and more completely than in cooler climates. Mark plant locations to avoid disturbing dormant roots. The golden foliage is most vivid in spring emergence; overplanting with summer annuals conceals the dormant gap.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil — bleeding hearts require consistent moisture and cool root zone conditions; Gold Heart is a cultivar of the classic bleeding heart (Lamprocapnos spectabilis, formerly Dicentra spectabilis) selected for its exceptional golden-yellow foliage — brilliant warm gold in spring when it first emerges from dormancy — that provides a dramatic contrast to the arching, pendant, rosy-pink heart-shaped flowers unique to bleeding hearts. While standard bleeding heart has conventional green foliage, Gold Heart's golden leaves transform the plant into a dual-feature specimen: an ornamental foliage plant even before the flowers appear and a striking flower display when the long, arching stems are laden with pendant heart-shaped blooms in April–May. The flowers have a characteristic two-outer-petal-over-two-inner-petal structure creating the perfect heart silhouette with a protruding white tip — one of the most instantly recognizable and beloved flower forms in the temperate garden. Gold Heart performs well throughout zone 9 as a spring ephemeral but requires excellent drainage to prevent crown rot during summer dormancy in hot, humid conditions.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Lamprocapnos spectabilis Gold Heart Award of Garden Merit
Missouri Botanical Garden — Bleeding Heart Varieties
North Carolina State University Extension — Shade Perennials for Southeast

planting.note: Plant container-grown hellebores in fall or early spring; in zone 9 plant October–February. Hellebores prefer consistent growing locations and resent transplanting — choose the final planting site carefully, as established plants bloom better when left undisturbed for years. In zone 9 hellebores perform best in deep shade under deciduous trees where they receive winter sun (when leaves are off the tree) and summer shade (protecting from intense heat). Mulch heavily to maintain cool, moist root conditions in summer. Anna's Red flowers December–March in zone 9, providing winter garden interest when almost nothing else blooms.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral, consistently moist soil — hellebores require cool root conditions and good drainage to prevent crown rot; Anna's Red is a named Lenten rose hybrid (Helleborus x hybridus) selected for its deep, rich, plum-red to burgundy-red nodding flowers that appear in the depths of winter — blooming December through March in zone 9 when virtually nothing else in the garden is flowering. Hellebores are among the most valuable shade garden plants precisely for their timing: the large, nodding, cup-shaped flowers open during the coldest, darkest weeks of the growing year, providing garden interest and early pollinator forage for bumblebees venturing out on warm winter days. The evergreen, leathery, deeply cut dark green leaves provide year-round structure in shade gardens even when plants are not in bloom. Anna's Red, like other Helleborus x hybridus selections, is the result of hybridization work that intensified and diversified the color range of Lenten roses far beyond the limited pink-purple range of the wild species. The nodding flower habit (flowers face downward) is traditional for hellebores and requires getting low to the ground to fully appreciate the beautiful interior of the flower — a charming quirk that makes hellebore viewing an intimate garden experience.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Hellebore for Southern Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Lenten Rose for Gulf South Shade
University of Georgia Extension — Helleborus for Zone 8-9

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown plants after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. Mona Lavender is a short-day bloomer — it flowers from late summer through fall when day length shortens, so plants grown through the long days of spring and summer may not flower until September–October in zone 9. In zone 10 it behaves as a year-round perennial; in zone 9 it may die back in cold winters but often resproutes from roots in spring. Take cuttings in fall to overwinter indoors. The deep purple undersides of the leaves are a distinctive feature even when the plant is not in bloom.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Mona Lavender performs well in the dappled light under trees and in north-facing shade gardens; Mona Lavender is a hybrid plectranthus (a member of the mint family, Lamiaceae) developed at Kirstenbosch National Botanical Garden in South Africa and introduced to the trade as one of the best ornamental shade plants for warm climates. The plant has a striking two-sided leaf appearance: the upper surface is deep, glossy, almost black-green with slightly embossed venation, while the underside is richly saturated deep purple — creating an unexpected color reveal when the leaves catch a breeze or are viewed from below. The flowers, which appear in late summer and fall when day length shortens, are long, elegant spikes of small lavender-purple blooms with a distinct, subtle fragrance. As a heat-tolerant shade plant that performs in zone 9's challenging combination of heat and shade, Mona Lavender fills a niche occupied by few other ornamental plants — providing substantial foliar and floral interest in the difficult conditions of partial to deep shade in humid subtropical climates where most traditional shade perennials struggle.
sources: Kirstenbosch National Botanical Garden — Mona Lavender Introduction
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Plectranthus for Florida Shade Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Shade Plants for Gulf Coast Landscapes

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant September–March. Bowles Vinca Minor is less aggressive than Vinca major (greater periwinkle) — a more restrained spreader that fills in shaded areas without the invasive tendency of its larger relative. In zone 9 the plant remains semi-evergreen through mild winters and spreads gradually via rooting stems. Unlike Vinca major, which can become invasive in mild climates, Vinca minor spreads slowly enough to be manageable in most garden situations. An excellent choice for shaded slopes, under trees, and in areas where conventional lawn grass fails.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile to lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Vinca minor tolerates a wide range of conditions and is one of the most reliable shade ground covers for difficult areas; Bowles is a selected cultivar of lesser periwinkle (Vinca minor) with larger flowers than the typical species — the lavender-blue, five-petaled, pinwheel-shaped flowers are noticeably larger (0.75–1 inch across) than standard Vinca minor, making Bowles the showiest of the periwinkle cultivars for ornamental impact in shade gardens. The glossy, small, dark green leaves form a dense, weed-suppressing mat 4–6 inches high that spreads gradually by rooting stems — creating a low-maintenance evergreen carpet under trees and in shaded slopes where lawn grass and most ground covers fail. Unlike the invasive Vinca major, Vinca minor is generally considered manageable in most garden settings, spreading at a modest rate that allows easy control by edging. The spring bloom (April–May) covers the established mat in periwinkle-blue flowers; occasional reblooming occurs in fall. Vinca minor is one of the most reliably effective ground covers for dry shade under mature trees — tolerating the combination of shade and root competition that defeats most other plants.
sources: Missouri Botanical Garden — Vinca minor Cultivar Selection
North Carolina State University Extension — Ground Covers for Shade
University of Georgia Extension — Shade Ground Covers for the South

planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Cora series annual vinca has superior disease resistance to Phytophthora aerial blight — a serious fungal disease of annual vinca (Catharanthus) in hot, humid conditions like zone 9's summer — making it significantly more reliable in the Gulf South than older vinca varieties. In zone 9 annual vinca is one of the most heat-tolerant and long-blooming summer bedding plants available; Cora Cherry thrives in the hottest, most humid summer weeks when most other annuals decline. The plant is self-cleaning — old flowers drop off without deadheading, keeping the plant tidy throughout the season.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — annual vinca thrives in heat and performs poorly in cool, wet, or poorly drained conditions; Cora Cherry is an All-America Selections award-winning annual vinca (Catharanthus roseus) from the Cora series, producing vivid, deep cherry-red, five-petaled flowers with a contrasting white eye that bloom continuously from planting until frost without deadheading. Annual vinca (technically not a true vinca but sharing the common name due to similar flower appearance) is among the top performers for hot, humid summer bedding in zone 9 — it genuinely thrives in tropical heat and actually improves in flowering intensity as temperatures rise. The Cora series was specifically bred for resistance to Phytophthora aerial blight, a fungal disease that devastates vinca plantings in humid summer conditions typical of the Gulf South; this disease resistance makes Cora dramatically more reliable than older annual vinca varieties in zone 9. The intense cherry-red flowers and the plant's exceptional summer performance make Cora Cherry one of the best red bedding annuals available for Gulf Coast summer gardens where heat and humidity challenge most alternatives.
sources: All-America Selections — Catharanthus Cora Cherry Award
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Vinca for Florida Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Heat-Tolerant Annuals for Gulf South
